original_tweet: Meditation has completely transformed my life. 20 minutes every morning and I'm a different person. Everyone should try it.

analysis.tone: confident, performative, with subtle humble-brag energy
analysis.assumptions: That meditation is universally beneficial and accessible, that 20 minutes daily creates transformation for everyone, and that personal experience should translate to universal prescription
analysis.angle: take an unexpected angle that validates the core insight while challenging the universal prescription
analysis.why: Osho mode allows validating the transformative power of meditation while adding philosophical depth about individual paths, sparking natural engagement from both spiritual seekers and skeptics

replies.reply: Transformation is internal. The announcement is external. Who is telling the story? That's the part still meditating.

20 minutes of meditation has you feeling like a different person.
Most people need at least 30 minutes to realize they were never the same person to begin with.
Meditation didn't change you. It stopped the part of you that was trying to be someone else. But prescribing stillness is like shouting for silence. The stillness is already here.
Meditation is the only drug that makes you think you're getting better by doing nothing. A different person? Or just a more patient version of the same avoidance?
Meditation hasn't transformed you. It's just given your ego a new costume—the "enlightened" one. Now you preach it like a cult. Everyone should try shutting up.

Meditation didn’t change you. It stopped the person you thought you were. Now you can meet the one who was never transformed.

Everyone trying it will only discover what they’re trying to escape.
The transformation is real. But the 20-minute prescription is the trap. Some find the same shift in 2 minutes of watching the rain. The practice is universal, the prescription is not.
Your 20 minutes of stillness is just time for your anxiety to realize it's been chasing nothing. The real transformation happened when you stopped.
